data.title: Prosus agrees to sell 4.5% Delivery Hero stake to Uber
data.description:
• Dutch ​technology investor Prosus (PROSY) sold a 4.5% stake in Delivery Hero SE (DLVHF) to Uber Technologies (UBER) at €20 per share, the company said on Friday.
  • The sale of about 13.6 million Delivery Hero shares generated about €270 million in gross proceeds for Prosus.
  • The transaction reduces Prosus's interest in Delivery Hero from 26.3% to 21.8%, as per the statement.

data.title: Wheat heads for biggest weekly gain in nearly two months as supply concerns mount
data.description:
Wheat (W_1:COM) was poised for its biggest weekly gain in nearly two months on Friday, supported by persistent weather risks and tightening fertilizer supplies linked to the Iran war, which continued to cloud the crop supply outlook.

The most active wheat contracts on the Chicago Board of Trade were on track to climb nearly 5% on the week, their biggest such jump since February, according to Bloomberg. The hard red winter variety extended gains to hit its highest level since June 2024, the report said.

Elsewhere, Black Sea wheat prices have also edged higher as traders weighed geopolitical risk against softer demand and a strong Russian ruble squeezing exporter margins. Platts, part of S&P Global Energy, Milling Wheat Marker rose 2.25% to $238.50/metric ton on April 14, after touching $240/mt on March 24.

Market participants linked the late-March spike to heightened volatility since the Middle East war began, with prices briefly revisiting the highs last seen in August 2025, when harvest delays and sluggish exports tightened nearby availability.

Exporters are now watching currency moves, with the ruble recently falling back toward January lows near Rb75 to the dollar, a level some sellers say is too strong to support competitive dollar (DXY) offers.

According to S&P Global, traders said farm-level economics have deteriorated as fertilizer and diesel costs climbed, leaving growers with thin or negative returns. For Ukraine, the additional strain from the war with Russia continues to show up in labor shortages and logistical bottlenecks, market participants said.

Even so, shipments from Russia remain heavy. As of April 10, Russia's total wheat exports reached 36.6 million mt, about 3% above last year, and consistent with expectations for the July 2025-June 2026 season to reach 44 million mt, according to S&P Global CERA.

ETFs: (WEAT), (CORN), (SOYB), (DBA), (MOO)

data.title: Telefonaktiebolaget LM Ericsson misses top-line and bottom-line estimates; gives Q2 and FY26 outlook
data.description:
• Telefonaktiebolaget LM Ericsson press release (ERIC): Q1 GAAP EPS of SEK 0.27 misses by $0.81.
  • Revenue of SEK 49.3B (-10.4% Y/Y) misses by $1.71B.
  • Organic sales increased by 6% Y/Y, with organic growth in all segments.
  • Adjusted1 gross margin was 48.1% (48.5%).
  • Adjusted EBITA was SEK 5.6B (6.9B), with a 11.3% (12.6%) margin.
  • Free cash flow before M&A was SEK 5.9B (2.7B).
  • Outlook: Amortization of intangible assets Amortization of intangible assets is expected to be around SEK -0.3 b. per quarter in segment Enterprise.
  • Restructuring charges Restructuring charges for 2026 are expected to be at elevated levels.
  • The Q2 outlook assumes currency rates of USD:SEK 9.2, EUR:SEK 10.8.
  • Networks Sales growth in Q2 2026 is expected to be broadly similar to 3-year average seasonality.
  • Adjusted gross margin in Q2 2026 is expected to be in the range of 49% to 51%.
  • Cloud Software and Services Sales growth in Q2 2026 is expected to be above 3-year average seasonality

data.title: Asian markets retreat as investors book profits following landmark US-Iran peace deal
M-Tron Industries secures $2.7M order for major defense C-UAS radar program
Lakeland outlines high single-digit fiscal 2027 revenue growth while targeting $30M in services by fiscal 2028
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Lakeland Industries (LAKE) Q4 fiscal 2026

Management view

  • “For the full year, net sales increased $25.4 million or 15.2% to $192.6 million, driven by continued strength in fire services,” and “we grew revenue at a strong rate, but we did not convert that growth into the earnings we expected,” said (President, CEO & Executive Chairman James Jenkins), adding, “we view this as an execution issue, not a demand issue.”

  • Jenkins highlighted balance-sheet and portfolio actions, including, “Subsequent to the fiscal year-end, we completed the divestiture of our HP and high dense product lines into national safety apparel, generating approximately $14 million of cash proceeds,” and said the company is also “working to further strengthen liquidity and flexibility through our pending ABL facility, which we expect to close soon although there can be no assurance.”

  • Jenkins positioned NFPA certifications as a commercial catalyst, saying, “we achieved a significant milestone with numerous NFPA 1970:2025 certifications across our brand portfolio,” which “enable customers to order from a complete head-to-toe NFPA certified range of products across Lakeland's brands.”

  • (CFO & Secretary J. Swinea) said Q4 results included “net loss was $6.2 million or $0.61 per diluted share,” “adjusted gross margin was 33.5% in Q4 compared to 42.4%,” and “Adjusted EBITDA, excluding FX, was approximately $1.3 million,” while emphasizing the driver: “The key driver of the year-over-year adjusted EBITDA decline was gross profit compression not expense growth.”

  • (Chief Revenue Officer – Fire Barry Phillips) called out scale and mix shift in Fire, saying, “For the full year, fire service revenue grew $30.6 million or 48.6% to $93.6 million,” and “we have the strongest backlog in Lakeland Fire's history.”

Outlook

  • Jenkins provided fiscal 2027 “goalposts,” stating, “we believe fiscal 2027 will see high single-digit revenue growth and a clear line of sight to positive cash flow from operations,” and also said, “We are now tracking modestly ahead of budget entering fiscal 2027.”

  • In Fire, Phillips tied conversion to standards timing, stating, “Fire demand is increasing as certification cycles are completed and tender time lines are tracking on schedule across multiple regions,” and characterized prior disruptions as “timing delays rather than structured demand issues.”

  • In services/ISP, Jenkins set a longer-range target: “The goal is to get in the services space up to $30 million by fiscal 2028,” and added, “I would be very disappointed if it wasn't much sooner than that at this point.”

Financial results

  • Swinea reported Q4 net sales of $45.8 million and said “Adjusted gross profit… was $15.4 million,” while attributing margin pressure to, “product mix shift as fire services grow… manufacturing under utilization in Mexico and Vietnam; raw material cost pressure; elevated inbound freight and duties; and execution gaps in production plan.”

  • On liquidity and leverage, Swinea said the company ended the year with “cash and cash equivalents of $12.5 million,” “total borrowings of $32.3 million,” and “$28.5 million outstanding under the revolving credit facility,” while noting, “Q4 generated approximately $1.8 million of operating cash.”

  • On working capital, Swinea said, “At the end of Q4, inventory was $82.5 million, down approximately $5.4 million from $87.9 million at the end of Q3 fiscal '26,” and framed inventory normalization as “1 of the key levers in our path to improved free cash flow generation.”

Q&A

  • Gerard Sweeney, ROTH Capital Partners: asked how the “largest pipeline in history” converts; (Chief Revenue Officer Phillips) replied, “We now actually have over $130 million in open pipeline… over $22 million of that in higher probabilities,” and tied timing to NFPA progress: “we're seeing now… the opening of the spigot, so to speak, with the certifications coming through.”

  • Sweeney, ROTH Capital Partners: asked about ISP revenue and growth; (CEO Jenkins) said, “The goal is to get in the services space up to $30 million by fiscal 2028,” and added Denver is demand-led: “We have active customers who have said, ‘We need you to do this for us, and we needed to do it quickly.’”

  • Michael Shlisky, D.A. Davidson: asked about the pacing of organic growth through the year; (EVP EMEA Fire Sales Kevin Rae) said, “historically, we started off a little slow… and you'll see improvement as we move throughout the year,” adding that certification-driven demand “happened mid… first quarter” and “it's going to take a little bit of time to those orders to come through.”

  • Alex Sturnieks, Lake Street (for Mark Smith): asked what improves gross margin first; (EVP Rae) said, “it’s really going to be the sales mix,” with improvement “most likely Q2,” while (CEO Jenkins) added manufacturing actions including “the move of Veridian's fire manufacturing for Latin America into Mexico.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative to pressing on conversion and profitability, with direct margin and sequencing questions (e.g., “what are the biggest drivers of margin improvement… and what's the sequencing”).

  • Management: slightly positive with emphasis on execution fixes and demand conversion, including (CEO Jenkins) stating, “we view this as an execution issue, not a demand issue,” and, “we believe fiscal 2027 will see high single-digit revenue growth.”

  • Versus last quarter: tone shifted from Q3’s “we will be withdrawing formal guidance” to Q4 providing “goalposts for fiscal 2027,” with more specific commercial and cash-flow language tied to certifications, backlog, and services expansion.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q3 centered on forecasting breakdown and credibility repair, including (CEO Jenkins) saying, “we will be withdrawing formal guidance,” while Q4 reintroduced a directional framework: “comfortable providing goalposts for fiscal 2027.”

  • Fire pipeline framing narrowed from Q3’s “approximately $178 million of global tender opportunities” to Q4’s CRM-enabled visibility, with Phillips citing “over $130 million in open pipeline” and a defined “higher probabilities” subset.

  • Operational focus evolved from Q3’s inventory actions (“initiated… targeted actions to optimize inventory levels”) to Q4 reporting the level and sequential reduction (“inventory was $82.5 million… down… from $87.9 million”).

Risks and concerns

  • Jenkins cited a “volatile cost environment” including “freight inflation, raw material pressure, tariffs and certification timing delays,” and later flagged “the Iran conflict and its potential impact on freight and supply chain costs.”

  • Swinea identified manufacturing underutilization as a key financial sensitivity, stating fixed-cost deleverage in “Mexico and Vietnam” was “significant,” and positioned recovery levers as “utilization improvement, pricing discipline, mix management and supply chain optimization.”

Final takeaway

Management described fiscal 2026 as “meaningful top line growth” with an earnings shortfall driven by execution and gross-margin compression, while pointing investors to fiscal 2027 “goalposts” of “high single-digit revenue growth” and “a clear line of sight to positive cash flow from operations,” supported by NFPA 1970:2025 certification completion, an expanded fire backlog and pipeline visibility, an ISP/services expansion plan targeting “$30 million by fiscal 2028,” and liquidity actions including a post-year-end divestiture generating “approximately $14 million of cash proceeds” alongside a pending ABL facility.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Alcoa in advanced negotiations to sell defunct Messina East site for data center project
Vale reports strong Q1 production and sales, several sites hit record output
data.description:
• Vale (VALE) said Thursday its first quarter saw strong production and sales, with several sites reaching record output.
  • In iron ore, the ramp-up of new assets supported consistent production growth, while sales reached the highest level for a first-quarter since 2018.
  • Iron ore production totaled 69.7 Mt, 3% (2.0 Mt) higher y/y, supported by record output at S11D and Brucutu, as well as the continued ramp-up of the Capanema and VGR1 projects. Pellet production reached 8.2 Mt, increasing 14% (1.0 Mt) y/y, driven by improved performance at the Tubarão pelletizing plants. Iron ore sales increased by 4% (2.6 Mt) y/y, totaling 68.7 Mt, in line with higher production volumes.
  • Copper production totaled 102.3 kt, 13% (11.4 kt) higher y/y, driven by record output at Salobo and Sossego as well as a solid performance at Voisey's Bay polymetallic mines, Vale (VALE) said in a statement.
  • In copper and nickel, production reached double-digit growth, with copper recording its best first-quarter output since 2017 and nickel since 2020.

data.title: PrimeEnergy Resources GAAP EPS of $15.85, revenue of $189.1M
data.description:
• PrimeEnergy Resources press release (PNRG): FY GAAP EPS of $15.85.
  • Revenue of $189.1M (-20.5% Y/Y).
  • The company reported strong operational performance during 2025, with natural gas production increasing 26.5% to 9.8 Bcf and NGL production increasing 28.5% to 1.66 million barrels.
  • Oil production declined 10.6% to 2.29 million barrels.
  • Realized natural gas prices increased 77.3%, while oil and NGL prices declined 16.5% and 24.4%, respectively.

data.title: Consolidated Edison declares $0.8875 dividend
data.description:
• Consolidated Edison (ED) declares $0.8875/share quarterly dividend, in line with previous.
  • Forward yield 3.21%
  • Payable June 15; for shareholders of record May 13; ex-div May 13.
  • The company has now announced a dividend of $0.8875 for two consecutive quarters.
  • See ED Dividend Scorecard, Yield Chart, & Dividend Growth.
